message.content: Can you tell me if OSC-8 is opinionated about the color of the text that one clicks to open a link?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

content.text:
No. OSC-8 is purely semantic — it associates a span of text with a URL. It says nothing about how that text should look.

The text between the opening `\e]8;;URL\e\\` and closing `\e]8;;\e\\` can carry any ANSI SGR styling you want (color, bold, underline, etc.), or none at all. OSC-8 doesn't override or constrain it.

What the *terminal emulator* does visually is its own business — most will add an underline or color on hover, some always underline, some do nothing until you Cmd/Ctrl-click. But that's the terminal's rendering policy, not the spec's.

file.content:
# buc_link "Prefix text" "Link text" "URL"  (prints to stderr like other user-visible messages)
buc_link() {
  local z_prefix="${1:-}"
  local z_text="${2:-}"
  local z_url="${3:-}"

  zbuc_tag_args 3 "buc_link    " "${z_prefix} ${z_text} -> ${z_url}"

  # Always show at verbosity >= 0 (same visibility as buc_step)
  if test "${BUC_VERBOSE:-0}" -ge 0; then
    # Print prefix text if provided
    test -n "${z_prefix}" && printf '%s ' "${z_prefix}" >&2

    # Print formatted hyperlink
    zbuc_hyperlink "${z_text}" "${z_url}" >&2
    echo >&2
  fi
}

# eof

content.text:
`zbuc_hyperlink` (line 386) hardcodes **blue underline** — `\033[34m\033[4m]` — wrapping the OSC-8 sequence. So the link text is always blue+underlined regardless of context.

This is a stylistic choice by `buc_link`, not something OSC-8 demands. The SGR codes are applied *around* the OSC-8 open/close, so the terminal sees both the color instruction and the hyperlink association.

The fallback path (when `BURD_NO_HYPERLINKS=1`) uses the same blue+underline but appends the raw URL in angle brackets instead of using OSC-8.

message.content: We talk a lot about SLSA provenance in README.md; tell me how many times the idea comes up.  Can you phrase me a concise definition why this is important for container image builds?  Websearch and/ or read RBS0 for the answer.

content.content: 5:- **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional egress-locked builds and supply-chain provenance 11:> The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 31:[Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 62:Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 65:The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 101:The [Director](#Director) manages the image lifecycle: [Ordain](#Ordain) a build, [Tally](#Tally) registry health, [Rekon](#Rekon) raw tags, [Vouch](#Vouch) provenance, [Abjure](#Abjure) superseded artifacts, and [Jettison](#Jettison) individual tags. 107:The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use, [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance, or [Wrests](#Wrest) a specific image directly. 118:[Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. 130:| [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | 154:After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. 163:- SLSA provenance attestation and verification 173:The distinctive case [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. 199:To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 274:Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the [Depot](#Depot) registry with associated provenance metadata. 277:[Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. 282:Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. 307:The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. 314:**[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain.

content.content:
99::rbtgr_provenance:          <<rbtgr_provenance,Rubric Provenance>>
782:**Exception — vouch provenance fetch**: The {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verification step (conjure mode only) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance` for SLSA provenance envelope retrieval.
785:The provenance fetch runs as Python 3 on the gcloud builder image after the primary image is built; the reproducibility concern that motivates this premise is weaker for post-build verification.
794:The vouch verification step (conjure mode) additionally invokes `gcloud` for provenance retrieval (see exception above).
1228:Bind and graft hallmarks are always vouchable; conjure hallmarks require SLSA provenance pre-check.
1602:build transcript, SBOM, and provenance data stored in the
1608:Shared fields include `vessel_mode`, `vessel_name`, `platform`, `image_digest`; conjure adds SLSA provenance fields; bind adds `bind_source`; graft adds `graft_source`.
1636:* *Conjure*: A single `builds.create` {gcb_service} job builds the image (context delivered via {rbtga_ark_pouch}) and embeds about steps (via stitch), producing both `-image` and `-about`. Then {rbtgo_ark_vouch} verifies SLSA provenance via DSSE envelope signature verification in a separate Cloud Build job.
1688:|SLSA verification record
1700:|Per-platform provenance-carrying images pushed via CB `images:` field
1707:The `-attest-«ARCH»` tags satisfy the {rbtgr_build_json} `images:` field for SLSA provenance generation.
1708:Provenance is keyed by digest in the Container Analysis API, but the `-attest-«ARCH»` tags are the only objects carrying those digests — the classic Docker image store re-serializes manifests on pull/push, producing different digests than the buildx-native manifests in `-image`.
1728:* *Conjure*: built by {gcb_service} from a Dockerfile. SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance is auto-generated.
1729:* *Bind*: mirrored from a pinned upstream registry reference. No SLSA provenance (upstream image, not locally built).
1730:* *Graft*: locally built and pushed via native docker/podman push. No SLSA provenance (no Cloud Build involvement).
1732:**Single-arch vessels**: a single-platform image. For conjure, pushed via the `images:` field with SLSA provenance.
1735:SLSA provenance is generated per platform via durable `-attest-«ARCH»` tags in the {rbtgr_build_json} `images:` field.
1736:The manifest list itself has `slsa_build_level: unknown` (expected — Cloud Build did not build the manifest list artifact); consumers pulling the `-image` tag get transparent platform resolution.
1737:The `-attest-«ARCH»` tags carry the only digests with GCB provenance (classic Docker image store re-serializes manifests, producing different digests than buildx-native).
1757:conjure `slsa` object — `build_level`, `build_invocation_id`, `provenance_predicate_types`, `provenance_builder_id`;
1782:* *Conjure*: DSSE envelope signature verification of GCB-signed provenance against each per-platform digest. Records per-platform verification verdicts.
1783:* *Bind*: Digest-pin comparison — verifies the image digest in GAR matches the pinned upstream reference. No SLSA provenance exists for upstream images.
1784:* *Graft*: GRAFTED stamp — records that the image was locally built and pushed without provenance chain. An explicit trust boundary marker.
1793:Provenance signatures verified against embedded GCB attestor public keys from `projects/verified-builder` KMS.
1795:Step 2 (gcloud/Python 3) fetches provenance via `gcloud artifacts docker images describe --show-provenance`, extracts the v1.0 DSSE envelope, decodes base64url payload + signature, reconstructs PAE, and verifies via `openssl dgst -sha256 -verify` subprocess against the embedded attestor key.
1796:No slsa-verifier, no jq — Python `json` + `base64` modules + openssl subprocess only.
1801:The vouch step branches on `_RBGV_VESSEL_MODE`: conjure runs DSSE provenance verification, bind runs digest-pin comparison, graft stamps GRAFTED.
1808:  * **gcloud exception**: Vouch verification (conjure mode) invokes `gcloud artifacts docker images describe` for provenance retrieval (see {rbsk_no_gcloud} exception).
1814:  * **DSSE verification**: Provenance envelope signatures verified against embedded GCB attestor public keys (`projects/verified-builder` KMS). PAE reconstruction + `openssl dgst -sha256 -verify` subprocess. No slsa-verifier, no jq — Python 3 standard library only.
1815:    builds.create provenance achieves SLSA Build Level 3 (₢AvAAB research confirmed).
1819:For conjure and bind, the new vouch content should match the existing vouch (same digests + same provenance/pin + same verifier = identical output).
1821:A mismatch for conjure/bind indicates provenance change or tampering and must produce a loud alarm.
1824:**Vouch provenance resolution**: The vouch verifier resolves per-platform digests from `-attest-«ARCH»` tags (HEAD request per tag) rather than from the `-image` manifest index.
1825:The `-attest-«ARCH»` digests are the GCB-attested digests; the `-image` manifest's per-platform digests are buildx-native and carry no GCB provenance.

1923://axl_voices axig_slsa_provenance
1924:{rbtgr_provenance}::
1925:SLSA v1.0 build provenance attestation auto-generated by Google for `builds.create` submissions.
1930:(1) `options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` — opts private pool builds into provenance generation (private pools default to no provenance);
1931:(2) the top-level `images:` field must list the image URI — Cloud Build generates provenance only for images it pushes via this field.
1935:The `images:` field in {rbtgr_build_json} causes Cloud Build to push the image and generate SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance.
1952:the `images:` field declares all `-attest-«ARCH»` tags — Cloud Build pushes each and generates independent SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance with the same `buildInvocationId`, proving single-build origin.
1954:The `-attest-«ARCH»` tags are **ephemeral attestation scaffolding**: they exist solely to bridge `buildx --push` output to the `images:` field for SLSA provenance generation.
1956:Provenance is keyed by digest in the Container Analysis API, not by tag.
1958:Deleting these tags does not delete provenance — the digests referenced by the `-image` manifest list retain their attestations.
1961:per-platform provenance (`b3fd60c7`) — all 3 platforms (amd64, arm64, armv7) achieved SLSA Level 3 with shared `buildInvocationId`;
1962:production e2e (`2e172ce0`) — build of `rbev-busybox` (3 platforms) achieved SLSA Level 3 on all per-platform images with shared `buildInvocationId`, consumer-facing manifest list resolved correctly.
1963:The manifest list itself has `slsa_build_level: unknown` (expected — Cloud Build did not build the manifest list artifact), but consumers pulling the `-image` tag get transparent platform resolution to attested per-platform digests with full SLSA Level 3.
1969:See `Memos/memo-20260305-provenance-architecture-gap.md` for full experiment details.
1982:`images:` lists `«HALLMARK»-attest-«ARCH»` tags — Cloud Build pushes each (idempotent if already present from step 5) and generates SLSA provenance.
1983:`options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` ensures build fails if provenance cannot be generated.
2186:Three vessel modes determine how images enter the {rbtgi_depot}: conjure (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), bind (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and graft (local push).
2519:// rbtc_refresh_binary_pins eliminated (₣Av) — slsa-verifier dropped
2529:Displays trust posture summary: vessel type, source, SLSA provenance, base image, build output, cache delta, SBOM summary, and vouch results.

1923://axl_voices axig_slsa_provenance
1924:{rbtgr_provenance}::
1925:SLSA v1.0 build provenance attestation auto-generated by Google for `builds.create` submissions.
1930:(1) `options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` — opts private pool builds into provenance generation (private pools default to no provenance);
1931:(2) the top-level `images:` field must list the image URI — Cloud Build generates provenance only for images it pushes via this field.
1935:The `images:` field in {rbtgr_build_json} causes Cloud Build to push the image and generate SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance.
1952:the `images:` field declares all `-attest-«ARCH»` tags — Cloud Build pushes each and generates independent SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance with the same `buildInvocationId`, proving single-build origin.
1954:The `-attest-«ARCH»` tags are **ephemeral attestation scaffolding**: they exist solely to bridge `buildx --push` output to the `images:` field for SLSA provenance generation.
1956:Provenance is keyed by digest in the Container Analysis API, not by tag.
1958:Deleting these tags does not delete provenance — the digests referenced by the `-image` manifest list retain their attestations.
1961:per-platform provenance (`b3fd60c7`) — all 3 platforms (amd64, arm64, armv7) achieved SLSA Level 3 with shared `buildInvocationId`;
1962:production e2e (`2e172ce0`) — build of `rbev-busybox` (3 platforms) achieved SLSA Level 3 on all per-platform images with shared `buildInvocationId`, consumer-facing manifest list resolved correctly.
1963:The manifest list itself has `slsa_build_level: unknown` (expected — Cloud Build did not build the manifest list artifact), but consumers pulling the `-image` tag get transparent platform resolution to attested per-platform digests with full SLSA Level 3.
1969:See `Memos/memo-20260305-provenance-architecture-gap.md` for full experiment details.
1982:`images:` lists `«HALLMARK»-attest-«ARCH»` tags — Cloud Build pushes each (idempotent if already present from step 5) and generates SLSA provenance.
1983:`options.requestedVerifyOption: VERIFIED` ensures build fails if provenance cannot be generated.
2186:Three vessel modes determine how images enter the {rbtgi_depot}: conjure (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), bind (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and graft (local push).
2519:// rbtc_refresh_binary_pins eliminated (₣Av) — slsa-verifier dropped
2529:Displays trust posture summary: vessel type, source, SLSA provenance, base image, build output, cache delta, SBOM summary, and vouch results.

content.text:
**In README.md**: the concept surfaces on **18 lines** — 6 explicit mentions of "SLSA" and 17 of "provenance" (with some overlap).

**Concise definition:**

SLSA provenance for container builds is a cryptographically signed record of *how* an image was produced — what source, what builder, what steps. It lets you verify that the image you're about to run actually came from the source you trust, built by infrastructure you control, and wasn't tampered with in transit or at rest. Without it, a container image is an opaque blob whose origin you take on faith.

RBS0 adds the practical detail: Google Cloud Build auto-generates SLSA v1.0 Level 3 provenance for images listed in the `images:` field of a build request, signing a DSSE envelope with KMS keys from `projects/verified-builder`. Recipe Bottle's vouch step then independently verifies that signature — Python stdlib + openssl only, no third-party verifier — closing the trust loop from source commit to running container.

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: 4 5 - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional egress-locked builds and supply-chain provenance 6 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" 7 8 > [!IMPORTANT] 9 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** 10 > 11 > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. 12 > 13 > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. 14 > 15 > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 16 17 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. 18 The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. 19 After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. 20 A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 21 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. 22 23 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 24 25

26 27

28 29 ## Environment 30 31 [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. 32 The two compose but neither requires the other. 33 34 All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. 35 Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). 36 Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). 37 38 Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. 39 The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. 40 Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. 41 Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. 42 43 Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). 44 Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. 45 Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. 46 47 A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. 48 Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. 49 50 The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. Commands that produce artifacts write them here. Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). 51 52 To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: 53 54 ``` 55 tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh 56 ``` 57 58 ## Foundry 59 60 [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. 61 The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. 62 Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). 63 Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. 64 65 The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. 66 Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 67 68 ### Depot 69 70 The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. 71 The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. 72 Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 73 74 Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: 75 76 - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. 77 - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. 78 79 ### Manor 80

# Recipe Bottle [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) provides two independent container image capabilities: - **[Foundry](#Foundry)**: orchestrate Google Cloud Build to produce multiplatform container images (x86 + ARM), fetch, retain, and serve them using a role-managed private cloud registry — with optional egress-locked builds and supply-chain provenance - **[Crucible](#Crucible)**: run untrusted containers behind enforced network isolation — DNS filtering and IP filtering — even using images unmodified from "in the wild" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome in both domains** > > The [Foundry's](#Foundry) egress-locked Cloud Build configuration — including the SLSA attestation chain, build isolation, and digest-pinned toolchains — has not yet had broad independent review. > > The [Crucible](#Crucible) runtime containment — a multi-container apparatus where the workload runs unprivileged in a network namespace it does not control — has also not had broad review, particularly the network isolation rules, privileged namespace setup, and egress enforcement. > > If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is a set of bash scripts enabling enterprise grade container image management intended for incorporation into any project. The dependency footprint is deliberately narrow — `bash 3.2` and a handful of standard tools — with no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, and no `gcloud` CLI. After initial manual setup, all cloud API calls use `openssl` + `curl`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) goal is a workflow where every container image has a verified origin and a controlled version, running behind appropriate network safeguards. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Environment [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) is organized around two independent capabilities: the [Foundry](#Foundry) builds container images with verifiable provenance, and the [Crucible](#Crucible) runs untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two compose but neither requires the other. All configuration flows through [Regimes](#Regime) — structured `.env` files with typed validation, each with its own render and validate commands. Some regimes are committed in the repo: [Vessel](#Vessel) definitions ([RBRV](#RBRV)), [Nameplate](#Nameplate) configurations ([RBRN](#RBRN)), [Depot](#Depot) identity ([RBRR](#RBRR)), and [Payor](#Payor) identity ([RBRP](#RBRP)). Others live on the filesystem outside revision control: OAuth credentials ([RBRO](#RBRO)), role credentials ([RBRA](#RBRA)), and developer workstation paths ([BURS](#BURS)). Every operation is launched through a [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) — a shell script in the `tt/` directory. The critical property: tab completion finds the command you want. Type `tt/rbw-` and the shell narrows to all [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) operations; type `tt/rbw-h` to see just the [Hallmark](#Hallmark) commands. Each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) is named `{colophon}.{frontispiece}.sh` — the colophon routes to the right module, the frontispiece tells you what it does. Every state-changing [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) writes three [Log](#Log) files to the directory named by `BURS_LOG_DIR` in your [BURS](#BURS) station file: a stable-name file (always the same path — easy for tooling to locate and evaluate the most recent run), a per-command file (same command name across runs — tools like SlickEdit sense diffs between executions), and a timestamped historical file (permanent record). Disk space is cheap; [Log](#Log) unconditionally so the diagnostic evidence is always there when something fails. Handbooks don't [Log](#Log) — teaching output is ephemeral. A [Transcript](#Transcript) is a single file capturing key decision points and state transitions within a [Tabtarget's](#Tabtarget) execution. Where [Logs](#Log) preserve full terminal output, a [Transcript](#Transcript) records the structured progress of sophisticated orchestration commands — the first thing to read when debugging a multi-step failure. The [Output](#Output) directory is a fixed-path staging area cleared and recreated before each [Tabtarget](#Tabtarget) runs. Commands that produce artifacts write them here. Concurrent bash sessions share this path, so parallel commands can overwrite each other's [Output](#Output). To begin, run the onboarding walkthrough: ``` tt/rbw-o.OnboardingStartHere.sh ``` ## Foundry [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) remote build orchestration system for producing, attesting, and distributing container images via Google Cloud Build and Google Artifact Registry. The [Foundry](#Foundry) manages [Depot](#Depot) access, [Vessels](#Vessel) choreography, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) tracking, and build definitions. Three [Vessel](#Vessel) modes determine how images enter the [Depot](#Depot): [Conjure](#Conjure) (egress-locked build from source with SLSA provenance), [Bind](#Bind) (digest-pinned upstream mirror), and [Graft](#Graft) (local push). Peer to [Crucible](#Crucible), which handles local runtime containment. The [Foundry](#Foundry) orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned [Depot](#Depot) registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. ### Depot The facility where container images are built and stored — has its own GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket, funded under the [Manor's](#Manor) billing account. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. Each [Depot](#Depot) supports two build egress profiles: - **[Tethered](#Tethered)** — Build egress mode allowing public internet access during Cloud Build. [Tethered](#Tethered) builds pull base images from upstream registries at build time — simpler to set up, but dependent on upstream availability. - **[Airgap](#Airgap)** — Build egress mode with no public internet access during Cloud Build. [Airgap](#Airgap) builds draw all dependencies from [Enshrined](#Enshrine) images in the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — fully self-contained, independent of upstream availability. Requires [Enshrining](#Enshrine) base images before the first build. ### Manor

content.content: 280 **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. 281 [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 282 Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. 283 284 **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. 285 [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. 286 This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 287 288 **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. 289 [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). 290 The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 291 292 **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. 293 The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. 294 This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. 295 296 ### Verification 297 298 **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. 299 [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. 300 The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 301 302 **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. 303 [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. 304 Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. 305 306 **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. 307 The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. 308 The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 309 310 **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and software bill of materials for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 311 The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the SBOM, build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. 312 Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. 313 314 **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. 315 [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. 316 Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 317 318 ### Distribution 319 320 **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. 321 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 322 323 **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. 324 [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. 325 Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. 326 327 ### Removal 328 329 **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. 330 [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. 331 The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. 332 333 **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. 334 [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. 335 Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. 336 337 ### Diagnostics 338 339 **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. 340 Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. 341 342 **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. 343 The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. 344 345 **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. 346 The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). 347 348 ## Appendix: Crucible Operations 349 350 Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. 351 352 ### Lifecycle 353 354 **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). 355 [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 356 357 **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. 358 [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 359 360 ### Interaction 361 362 **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. 363 [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. 364 365 **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. 366 [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. 367 368 **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. 369 [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. 370 371 ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method 372 373 The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: 374 375 - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. 376 - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. 377 378 The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. 379 The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. 380 Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. 381 The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. 382 383 ## Appendix: Roadmap 384 385 The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: 386 387 - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. 388 389 - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. 390 391 - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. 392 393 - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. 394 395 - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. 396 397 ## Appendix: Reference Project 398 399 This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). 400 The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. 401 402 | Path | Description | 403 |------|-------------| 404 | `Project Root/` | | 405 | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | 406 | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | 407 | `├── Tools/` | | 408 | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | 409 | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | 410 | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | 411 | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | 412 | `│ ��── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | 413 | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | 414 | `│ ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox [Crucible](#Crucible) | 415 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Claude Code, Anthropic-only allowlist | 416 | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [tadmor](#tadmor) adversarial testing [Crucible](#Crucible) | 417 | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit), restrictive allowlist | 418 | `│ ├── srjcl/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — Jupyter notebook [Crucible](#Crucible) | 419 | `│ │ └── file.content: **[Bind](#Bind)** — Mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. **[Graft](#Graft)** — Push a locally-built image to the [Depot](#Depot) registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though [About](#About) and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). **[Kludge](#Kludge)** — Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without [Depot](#Depot) registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. **[Pouch](#Pouch)** — Build context packaged as a FROM SCRATCH OCI image and pushed to the [Depot's](#Depot) registry before a Cloud Build job runs. The [Director](#Director) controls what enters the [Pouch](#Pouch) — Dockerfile, context files, build scripts — and the cloud receives only what the [Pouch](#Pouch) contains. This is the security boundary between workstation and build infrastructure. ### Verification **[Tally](#Tally)** — Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the [Depot](#Depot) registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. **[Rekon](#Rekon)** — Raw listing of image tags in the [Depot](#Depot) registry for a [Vessel](#Vessel) package. [Rekon](#Rekon) is a [Director](#Director)-only diagnostic that shows exactly what exists in the registry without health interpretation. Where [Tally](#Tally) groups [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by status, [Rekon](#Rekon) shows the unprocessed tag inventory. **[Vouch](#Vouch)** — Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. **[About](#About)** — Build metadata and software bill of materials for a [Hallmark](#Hallmark). The [About](#About) artifact (`-about` tag) contains the SBOM, build transcript, build configuration snapshot, and key package summaries — bundled as a compressed archive and stored as a Generic Artifact in GAR. Every [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an [About](#About) alongside the image. **[Plumb](#Plumb)** — Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). ### Distribution **[Summon](#Summon)** — Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). **[Wrest](#Wrest)** — Pull a specific image from the [Depot](#Depot) registry by reference. [Wresting](#Wrest) is a direct pull without [Vouch](#Vouch) verification — used when you need a specific image tag regardless of attestation status. Compare with [Summon](#Summon), which enforces the [Vouch](#Vouch) ceremony. ### Removal **[Abjure](#Abjure)** — Remove a [Hallmark's](#Hallmark) artifacts from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry — the `-image`, [`-about`](#About), and [`-vouch`](#Vouch) tags deleted as a coherent unit. [Abjuring](#Abjure) is the reverse of [Ordaining](#Ordain): it formally renounces a build instance. The [Director](#Director) [Abjures](#Abjure) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) that are superseded, broken, or no longer needed. **[Jettison](#Jettison)** — Delete a specific image tag from the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. [Jettisoning](#Jettison) is lower-level than [Abjure](#Abjure) — it removes a single tag rather than a complete [Hallmark](#Hallmark) artifact set. Used for cleanup of individual registry entries. ### Diagnostics **[List Depots](#ListDepots)** — Inventory all active [Depots](#Depot) visible to the current [Payor](#Payor) credentials. Shows project IDs, regions, and provisioning status. **[JWT Probe](#JWTProbe)** — Test service account authentication. The [JWT Probe](#JWTProbe) verifies that a [Governor](#Governor), [Director](#Director), or [Retriever](#Retriever) credential can successfully authenticate to the [Depot's](#Depot) GCP project — useful for diagnosing access failures after credential creation or rotation. **[OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe)** — Test [Payor](#Payor) OAuth authentication. The [OAuth Probe](#OAuthProbe) verifies that the stored refresh token can obtain a valid access token — useful for diagnosing [Payor](#Payor) operation failures before attempting a full [Refresh](#Refresh). ## Appendix: Crucible Operations Formal definitions for all [Crucible](#Crucible) operations. ### Lifecycle **[Charge](#Charge)** — Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. **[Quench](#Quench)** — Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). ### Interaction **[Rack](#Rack)** — Shell into a [Bottle](#Bottle) container. [Racking](#Rack) opens an interactive session inside the running workload — for debugging, inspecting state, or running commands as the [Bottle](#Bottle) user would experience them. **[Hail](#Hail)** — Shell into a [Sentry](#Sentry) container. [Hailing](#Hail) opens an interactive session on the gateway — for inspecting `iptables` rules, `dnsmasq` configuration, network state, and egress logs. **[Scry](#Scry)** — Observe network traffic across [Crucible](#Crucible) containers. [Scrying](#Scry) captures packets on the [Crucible's](#Crucible) network interfaces — for verifying that blocked traffic is actually blocked, diagnosing connectivity issues, or watching the [Sentry's](#Sentry) filtering in action. ## Appendix: Adversarial Test Method The [Crucible's](#Crucible) containment is validated through coordinated escape testing using two components: - **[Ifrit](#Ifrit)** — Adversarial attack [Vessel](#Vessel) purpose-built to run inside a [Bottle](#Bottle), seeking escape. The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) carries scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary probing), and a minimal footprint — tools chosen to probe every surface the [Sentry's](#Sentry) containment exposes. Named for the djinn imprisoned in a bottle. - **[Theurge](#Theurge)** — Test orchestrator running on the host, outside the [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Theurge](#Theurge) [Charges](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) with the [Ifrit](#Ifrit) as its [Bottle](#Bottle), then dispatches curated, reproducible, version-controlled attack scripts targeting specific surfaces: DNS exfiltration, ICMP covert channels, cloud metadata probing, namespace breakout, and direct IP bypass attempts. Each attack runs inside the [Bottle](#Bottle) while the [Theurge](#Theurge) simultaneously observes the [Sentry's](#Sentry) network from outside — confirming that blocked traffic is actually blocked, not merely unrequested. The escape tests were developed through adversarial Claude Code sessions with full visibility into the [Sentry's](#Sentry) source, configuration, and the [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle) specification. The [Ifrit](#Ifrit) [Vessel](#Vessel) is the delivery vehicle; the intelligence came from the authoring process. Every test that passes is evidence the containment holds — not proof. The test suite grows as new attack surfaces are identified. ## Appendix: Roadmap The following features are not yet implemented but are under consideration: - **VPC Service Controls** - Google Cloud security perimeters that prevent data from being copied out of a project even if an attacker holds valid credentials. [Recipe Bottle's](#RecipeBottle) Cloud Build architecture uses private pools, which are the prerequisite for VPC enforcement; enabling the controls themselves is deferred until organizational policy or external distribution requires them. - **Cosign container signing** - Cryptographic image signatures independent of registry trust. Deferred alongside VPC Service Controls until external distribution triggers the need. - **CDN-aware IP gating** - When allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse: DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN address ranges. A tighter mechanism is recognized but not yet designed. - **Podman support** - The spec accommodates Podman as an alternative container runtime, but support is deferred. On macOS, both Docker and Podman run Linux containers inside a hidden Linux VM — there is no native container runtime on Darwin. Podman support would require managing that VM's lifecycle within the customer's [Depot](#Depot), adding infrastructure complexity with no architectural advantage over Docker Desktop. - **[Crucible](#Crucible)-to-[Crucible](#Crucible) networking** - Under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model, [Bottles](#Bottle) have no direct network path to each other; any inter-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication would route through their respective [Sentries](#Sentry). The plumbing is feasible but not implemented, pending a concrete use case. ## Appendix: Reference Project This repository is the reference implementation of [Recipe Bottle](#RecipeBottle). The annotated tree below maps its files to the concepts defined above. | Path | Description | |------|-------------| | `Project Root/` | | | `├── CLAUDE.md` | [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) command reference, glossary, conventions | | `├── tt/` | 136 [Tabtargets](#Tabtarget) — `tt/rbw-` for all operations | | `├── Tools/` | | | `│ ├── buk/` | Bash Utility Kit — portable CLI infrastructure | | `│ └── rbk/` | Recipe Bottle Kit — domain logic | | `├── .buk/` | [BURC](#BURC) project structure [Regime](#Regime) | | `├── .rbk/` | [Regime](#Regime) configuration root | | `│ ��── rbrp.env` | [RBRP](#RBRP) — [Payor](#Payor) identity for this [Depot](#Depot) | | `│ ├── rbrr.env` | [RBRR](#RBRR) — [Depot](#Depot) identity and build configuration | | `│ ├── ccyolo/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [ccyolo](#ccyolo) Claude Code sandbox [Crucible](#Crucible) | | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + Claude Code, Anthropic-only allowlist | | `│ ├── tadmor/` | [Nameplate](#Nameplate) — [tadmor](#tadmor) adversarial testing [Crucible](#Crucible) | | `│ │ └── rbrn.env` | [RBRN](#RBRN) — [Sentry](#Sentry) + [Ifrit](#Ifrit),
